data.sentence: Alright, I think we've got most people on now — Hannah, you there?
Yeah, I'm here, sorry — just had another call run long.
No worries, Ananya and Alex are on, Nina just joined — okay good, let's just get started.
Morning everyone.
Hey Nina.
Okay so, uh, this is the April all-hands update — I sent the agenda doc around yesterday, hopefully everyone had a chance to at least skim it.
I looked at it this morning, yeah.
Same, I skimmed most of it.

Okay great. So I want to cover a few things — we'll do a quick retrospective on the March Detect outage, then talk about the Comply v2 launch, and then get into Q2 planning for the roadmap. Should take us under an hour.
Works for me.
So starting with Detect — Ravi, do you want to walk us through where we landed on the post-mortem?

Sure, yeah. So, uh, for those who maybe weren't as close to it — the outage ran roughly six hours in mid-March, March 10th through the 18th was the window where we were seeing instability, but the main event was that six-hour gap on the 10th where the event ingestion pipeline just... collapsed.
Right and that was a cascading failure, not a single point thing — or, well, it started as a single point of failure and then cascaded, right?

Exactly, yeah. The root cause was we had a single point of failure in the ingestion layer — one node goes down, nothing has a fallback, and then the backpressure just propagates upstream and the whole thing falls over.
And customers had zero visibility into their threat monitoring for that whole window?
Yeah, effectively. Alerts weren't firing, the dashboard was showing stale data — it was not great.
I know customer success had a rough week after that. I got looped into like three customer escalations.
So what's the fix look like at this point? I know we deployed something but I'm not clear on the details.

So we went with redundant processing nodes and we implemented the circuit breaker pattern on the ingestion side. Basically if one node degrades past a threshold, traffic gets rerouted before it can cascade. It's been stable since we pushed that.
Has QA validated that path? Like have we actually tested a node failure scenario end to end?
We did a tabletop sim, yeah. Not a full live failure test — that's something I want to get on the schedule actually, a chaos engineering run in staging.
I'd like to see that happen before end of May honestly. We should have that validation in place.
Agreed. Ravi, can you put together a scope doc for that?
Yeah I can do that. Probably mid-May for the actual run if we scope it out next week.
Okay good, let's track that. Moving on — Comply v2. Alex, you've been closest to this one from the product side.

Yeah so, uh, Comply v2 went GA on April 7th — on-demand reporting is live, we've got SOC 2, PCI DSS, HIPAA, and ISO 27001 all supported. The big differentiator here is the multi-framework support, which is something we've been hearing from customers for... a while.
How's the adoption looking so far? It's been, what, three weeks?

Almost three weeks, yeah. Early numbers are decent — we've had a solid chunk of existing Comply customers generate at least one on-demand report. The ISO 27001 one in particular, that was a gap for us before, and we're seeing uptake there.
What about new logo deals? Has it moved any pipeline?

Sales is pointing to a couple of deals where it was a factor — I don't want to overstate it, it's early. But the positioning against, say, VaultEdge on compliance reporting is cleaner now. That was always a weak spot.
Yeah VaultEdge was beating us on that pretty consistently in competitive deals from what I heard.
Okay, any known issues post-launch? Nina, anything from QA's perspective that's still open?

There are two open items — one is a formatting inconsistency on the PCI DSS export, it's a PDF rendering thing, not a data accuracy issue, but it looks sloppy. And then there's a minor edge case on the multi-framework run where if you queue more than three reports simultaneously it occasionally times out.
Is the timeout one reproducible consistently or is it intermittent?
Intermittent, maybe... two out of five tries? It's not consistent but it's frequent enough that we should fix it before we start pushing the concurrent reporting use case in marketing.
Okay that's fair. Ananya, can you take the timeout bug? And I'll get the PDF formatting thing assigned to someone on the front-end side.
Yeah I'll pick it up, I want to look at the queue handling anyway — there might be a broader issue there.

Alright. Let's jump into Q2 planning. So we've got a few things in flight — Identity module updates, some Detect improvements that were already on the roadmap pre-outage, and then we need to figure out where Protect fits into the next cycle.

On Detect — I want to flag that we should be careful about piling too much onto that module right now. The team just went through a really stressful period and we're still stabilizing. I'd rather we scope conservatively.
I hear you, and I think that's reasonable, but there are some roadmap items that have been deprioritized twice already — at some point we're going to have frustrated stakeholders.
I know, but pushing features at the expense of reliability work seems like the wrong call after what just happened.

Can we try to split the difference? Like, identify which Detect items are strictly feature work versus which ones have a reliability or observability angle — prioritize the latter and hold the pure feature stuff for Q3.
That actually works for me. I can live with that.
Yeah, I think that's a reasonable split. Let's document that logic so when leadership asks why certain items moved we have a rationale.
Good call. Okay — I think we've hit the major items. Any other blockers or things people want to flag before we close out?
Nothing from me.
I'm good.
Nope, I think we covered it.
Alright. I'll send out a summary with the action items — Ravi on the chaos engineering scope, Ananya on the timeout bug, and the Q2 prioritization framework. Thanks everyone.
Thanks Hannah.
Thanks, talk soon.
